meta.name: IES-R Impact of Event Scale - Revised
meta.description: The IES-R is a 22-item self-report measure that assesses subjective distress caused by traumatic events. It measures three dimensions of post-traumatic stress: intrusion, avoidance, and hyperarousal.
meta.author: Weiss & Marmar (1997)
meta.reference: Weiss DS, Marmar CR. The Impact of Event Scale-Revised. In: Wilson JP, Keane TM, eds. Assessing Psychological Trauma and PTSD. New York: Guilford Press; 1997:399-411.
meta.instruction: Below is a list of difficulties people sometimes have after stressful life events. Please read each item and then indicate how distressing each difficulty has been for you during the past 7 days with respect to the event you experienced.

questions.text: Any reminder brought back feelings about it
I had trouble staying asleep
Other things kept making me think about it
I felt irritable and angry
I avoided letting myself get upset when I thought about it or was reminded of it
I thought about it when I did not mean to
I felt as if it had not happened or was not real
I stayed away from reminders about it
Pictures about it popped into my mind
I was jumpy and easily startled
I tried not to think about it
I was aware that I still had a lot of feelings about it, but I did not deal with them
My feelings about it were kind of numb
I found myself acting or feeling like I was back at that time
I had trouble falling asleep
I had waves of strong feelings about it
I tried to remove it from my memory
I had trouble concentrating
Reminders of it caused me to have physical reactions, such as sweating, trouble breathing, nausea, or a pounding heart
I had dreams about it
I felt watchful and on guard
I tried not to talk about it

ranges.label: Normal Range
ranges.description: Your score indicates no clinically significant post-traumatic distress. The event does not appear to be causing ongoing psychological disruption to your daily life or emotional well-being.
suggestions: Continue using your current healthy coping strategies to process the experience
Allow yourself to work through the event at a comfortable pace without pressure
Stay connected with supportive friends and family members

ranges.label: Mild Impact
ranges.description: Your score suggests mild post-traumatic distress that may benefit from monitoring. Some intrusive thoughts, avoidance behaviors, or heightened arousal related to the event may be present.
suggestions: Monitor your symptoms over the next several weeks to track any changes in frequency or intensity
Incorporate regular self-care routines including adequate sleep, exercise, and relaxation practices
Talk openly with someone you trust about your experiences and feelings
Consider consulting a mental health professional if symptoms persist or worsen over time

ranges.label: Moderate Impact
ranges.description: Your score reflects moderate post-traumatic distress with a notable impact on your well-being. Intrusive memories, avoidance patterns, and hyperarousal symptoms may be interfering with daily functioning.
suggestions: Seek support from a qualified mental health professional experienced in trauma treatment
Practice grounding techniques such as deep breathing, progressive muscle relaxation, or the 5-4-3-2-1 sensory exercise
Maintain consistent daily routines for sleep, meals, and physical activity to provide stability
Avoid isolating yourself and make intentional efforts to stay socially connected
Limit exposure to triggering content in media or conversations when possible

ranges.label: Severe Impact
ranges.description: Your score indicates severe post-traumatic distress that is significantly affecting your psychological functioning. This level of symptoms is consistent with a possible PTSD diagnosis requiring professional evaluation.
suggestions: Please seek professional help from a therapist specializing in trauma-focused treatments such as EMDR or CPT
If you are in crisis, contact a crisis helpline immediately (e.g., 988 Suicide and Crisis Lifeline)
Develop a safety plan with a professional for managing episodes of intense distress
Follow through consistently with professional treatment recommendations and scheduled appointments
Reach out to trusted individuals who can provide immediate emotional support during difficult moments

intrusion.label: Low
intrusion.description: Intrusive re-experiencing symptoms are within the normal range. You are not significantly troubled by unwanted memories, flashbacks, or distressing dreams related to the event.

intrusion.label: Mild
intrusion.description: You are experiencing mild intrusion symptoms. Occasional unwanted thoughts, memories, or dreams about the event may surface but remain manageable in daily life.

intrusion.label: Moderate
intrusion.description: Intrusion symptoms are at a moderate level and may be disrupting your concentration, sleep, or emotional stability. Flashbacks or vivid memories of the event may occur with notable frequency.

intrusion.label: Severe
intrusion.description: Severe intrusion symptoms are significantly impairing your functioning. Persistent unwanted memories, intense flashbacks, and distressing nightmares may dominate your daily experience.

avoidance.label: Low
avoidance.description: Avoidance symptoms are within the normal range. You are not significantly restricting your activities or suppressing thoughts and feelings related to the traumatic event.

avoidance.label: Mild
avoidance.description: You show mild avoidance patterns. You may occasionally steer away from reminders, conversations, or places associated with the event, but this does not substantially limit your life.

avoidance.label: Moderate
avoidance.description: Avoidance behaviors are at a moderate level and may be limiting your activities and social engagement. You may actively avoid situations, people, or feelings connected to the event.

avoidance.label: Severe
avoidance.description: Severe avoidance is significantly restricting your daily life and relationships. Extensive efforts to avoid trauma-related thoughts, feelings, and situations may be narrowing your world considerably.

hyperarousal.label: Low
hyperarousal.description: Hyperarousal symptoms are within the normal range. Your sleep, concentration, and startle responses do not appear to be significantly affected by the traumatic event.

hyperarousal.label: Mild
hyperarousal.description: You are experiencing mild hyperarousal symptoms. Occasional sleep difficulties, irritability, or heightened vigilance may be present but remain at a manageable level.

hyperarousal.label: Moderate
hyperarousal.description: Hyperarousal symptoms are at a moderate level and may be noticeably affecting your daily functioning. Persistent irritability, concentration problems, and exaggerated startle responses are likely.

hyperarousal.label: Severe
hyperarousal.description: Severe hyperarousal symptoms are substantially impairing your daily life. Chronic sleep disturbance, intense irritability, hypervigilance, and exaggerated startle reactions require professional intervention.
